title: 10 best herbal teas for pertussis
intro_desc: Herbal teas for pertussis are infusions made from various dried herbs that can help alleviate symptoms of pertussis, also known as whooping cough. These teas can soothe a sore throat, reduce inflammation, and alleviate coughing spells. Examples of herbal teas that can be beneficial for pertussis include licorice root, thyme, and chamomile. Licorice root can help reduce inflammation and soothe a sore throat, while thyme has antimicrobial properties that can help fight off the bacteria that cause pertussis. Chamomile, on the other hand, has calming properties that can help promote relaxation and improve sleep, which can be especially helpful for individuals with pertussis who may be experiencing difficulty sleeping due to persistent coughing. By using these herbal teas, individuals with pertussis can find relief from their symptoms and improve their overall quality of life.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Purple giant hyssop teas helps with pertussis because it contains bioactive compounds that have been shown to exhibit antibacterial properties, which can help combat the Bordetella pertussis bacteria responsible for the disease. The tea's active ingredients, such as essential oils and flavonoids, have been found to inhibit bacterial growth and reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract, making it a potential adjunctive treatment option for individuals with pertussis.
remedy_properties: Antimicrobial activity: Purple giant hyssop tea has been shown to exhibit antimicrobial properties, which can help combat the bacterial infection caused by Bordetella pertussis, reducing the severity and duration of pertussis symptoms.

Anti-inflammatory properties: Hyssop tea's anti-inflammatory compounds may help alleviate coughing fits and reduce inflammation in the lungs and airways, making it easier to breathe and potentially shortening the duration of illness.

Expectorant action: The expectorant properties of purple giant hyssop tea can help loosen and clear mucus from the lungs and airways, reducing congestion and relieving coughing fits associated with pertussis.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots are rich in saponins, which have antibacterial and antispasmodic properties that can help alleviate coughing and ease respiratory congestion associated with pertussis.
Bark: The bark contains alkaloids that have anti-inflammatory and expectorant properties, making it effective in reducing inflammation and relieving congestion in the lungs and airways.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 2-3 cups of fresh or dried purple giant hyssop leaves and flowers in the early morning or evening.
Rinse the herb material with cool filtered water to remove any dirt or debris and pat it dry gently.
Combine 2 tablespoons of the prepared herb material with 1 quart of boiling water and let steep for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea mixture through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a large glass jar or teapot to remove the solids.
Store the cooled tea in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to 3 days or freeze for up to 6 months.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Hollyhock teas helps with pertussis because of its natural anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The tea's active compounds, such as flavonoids and terpenes, have been shown to effectively combat bacterial infections like pertussis by reducing inflammation in the lungs and sinuses. Additionally, the tea's soothing and calming effects can help alleviate coughing spasms and congestion, making it a natural remedy for relieving symptoms of pertussis.
remedy_properties: Antiviral activity: Hollyhock extracts have shown antiviral activity against certain viruses, including herpes simplex and influenza A, which could potentially help combat respiratory infections like pertussis.
Anti-inflammatory properties: The flavonoids present in hollyhock tea may help reduce inflammation and alleviate symptoms associated with respiratory conditions, such as coughing and congestion.
Antioxidant activity: Hollyhock tea is rich in antioxidants, which can help protect the body from oxidative stress and damage caused by free radicals, potentially supporting overall immune function.
remedy_parts: Roots: Containing alkaloids and glycosides, hollyhock roots have been traditionally used to treat respiratory issues, making them effective in soothing coughs and relieving pertussis symptoms.

Bark: The bark of the hollyhock plant contains flavonoids and phenolic acids, which help reduce inflammation and combat bacterial infections, making it a suitable ingredient for medicinal teas for pertussis.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried hollyhock flowers alcea digitata by cutting stems just before flowering or drying existing blooms.
Measure one teaspoon of dried hollyhock flowers or two teaspoons of fresh petals for every eight ounces of boiling water.
Heat filtered water to a boil in a teapot or kettle then remove from heat and let it steep for 5-7 minutes with the hollyhock flowers.
Strain the tea into a cup using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth and discard solids immediately after brewing.
Allow the tea to cool slightly before serving hot or refrigerate for at least two hours to allow flavors to meld and chill before serving.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Marshmallow teas helps with pertussis because of its soothing properties, which can provide relief from the persistent coughing that is a hallmark symptom of this respiratory infection. The mucilage in marshmallow root coats and protects the irritated mucous membranes in the throat, reducing inflammation and discomfort. Additionally, the anti-inflammatory compounds present in marshmallow tea may also help to ease congestion and open up airways, making it easier for the body to expel the infection.
remedy_properties: Mucilages: Marshmallow tea contains mucilages, which are thick, protective coatings that soothe and protect irritated mucous membranes in the throat and airways, providing relief from coughing fits and congestion caused by pertussis.

Antioxidants: Marshmallow tea is rich in antioxidants, such as flavonoids and phenolic acids, which help reduce inflammation and oxidative stress in the body, potentially reducing the severity of pertussis symptoms like chest pain and wheezing.

Expectorant properties: The expectorant compounds in marshmallow tea, including saponins and glycosides, help loosen and clear mucus from the airways, making it easier to cough up and breathe more easily during a pertussis infection.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in mucilage, a thick gel-like substance that helps soothe and calm respiratory issues such as pertussis.
Bark: Containing flavonoids and saponins, which have anti-inflammatory properties that aid in reducing inflammation and congestion associated with pertussis.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh marshmallow root and cut it into small pieces weighing about two ounces.
Combine the harvested root with four cups of cold water in a large saucepan.
Bring the mixture to a boil then reduce heat and let simmer for 20 minutes.
Strain the tea through a cheesecloth or fine-mesh sieve into a large pitcher.
Allow the tea to cool and steep for at least 30 minutes before serving.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Butterfly weed teas helps with pertussis because it contains alkaloids that have antimicrobial properties, which can help combat the bacterial infection caused by Bordetella pertussis. The tea's flavonoids and saponins may also help to reduce inflammation and alleviate symptoms such as coughing, fever, and chest pain associated with pertussis. Additionally, the tea's expectorant properties may help to loosen and clear mucus from the airways, making it easier for the body to expel the infection.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory compounds: The alkaloids present in Butterfly Weed tea, such as resiniferatoxin, have anti-inflammatory effects that may help reduce inflammation and congestion associated with pertussis.

Antimicrobial properties: The flavonoids, terpenoids, and other bioactive compounds in Butterfly Weed tea possess antimicrobial activity, which can help combat the bacterial infection caused by Bordetella pertussis, the bacteria responsible for pertussis.

Expectorant and bronchodilatory effects: The volatile oils and saponins in Butterfly Weed tea may act as a natural expectorant, helping to loosen and clear mucus from the lungs, while also relaxing airway spasms and reducing coughing fits characteristic of pertussis.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of the butterfly weed plant contain alkaloids, which have been shown to possess antibacterial properties that can help combat bacterial infections like pertussis.
Rhyzomes: The rhyzomes of the butterfly weed plant are rich in flavonoids and terpenoids, which have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial effects that may aid in relieving symptoms of pertussis.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried asclepias tuberosa leaves and stems in the morning for optimal potency.
Chop one ounce of herb into small pieces using scissors or a mortar and pestle for maximum release.
Steep one teaspoon of chopped herb in one cup of boiling water for 5 to 7 minutes to extract properties.
Strain tea leaves from liquid using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth to remove solids.
Discard solids and transfer tea to a clean container, ready to drink immediately or store refrigerated for up to 3 days.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Wild indigo teas helps with pertussis because it has been traditionally used to treat respiratory infections, including whooping cough. The tea's antibacterial properties help combat the Bordetella pertussis bacteria that causes the infection, reducing the severity and duration of symptoms such as coughing, congestion, and fever. Additionally, wild indigo's anti-inflammatory properties may help soothe irritated airways and reduce discomfort during coughing fits, allowing patients to recover more quickly.
remedy_properties: Antimicrobial properties: Wild Indigo tea's antimicrobial properties help combat the bacterial infection caused by Bordetella pertussis, the bacteria responsible for Pertussis.

Anti-inflammatory properties: The anti-inflammatory compounds in Wild Indigo tea reduce inflammation and congestion in the airways, making it easier to breathe and relieving symptoms like coughing and wheezing.
Antiviral properties: Wild Indigo tea's antiviral properties help prevent the spread of Pertussis by inhibiting the replication of the virus, reducing the severity and duration of the infection.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in bioactive compounds, indigo roots have been traditionally used to treat respiratory issues like pertussis due to their anti-inflammatory and antitussive properties.
Bark: Containing tannins and other bioactives, the bark of wild indigo is often used to make teas that help soothe and calm irritated respiratory passages associated with pertussis.
remedy_recipe: Harvest dried or fresh wild indigo roots and leaves, selecting material from organic sources whenever possible.
Chop the harvested plant material into small pieces, ensuring uniformity for efficient brewing.
Measure out one teaspoon of dried wild indigo root or two teaspoons of fresh plant material per eight ounces of boiling water.
Steep the chopped plant material in hot water for 10-15 minutes to extract its medicinal properties and flavor.
Strain the tea mixture through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth, discarding solids before serving warm or chilled as desired.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Daisy teas helps with pertussis because its calming properties can soothe the respiratory tract, alleviating the severe coughing spasms that are a hallmark symptom of this bacterial infection. The antimicrobial compounds found in herbal daisies, such as apigenin and luteolin, have been shown to exhibit potent antibacterial activity, which may help combat the Bordetella pertussis bacteria responsible for pertussis. Additionally, the tea's anti-inflammatory properties can reduce inflammation and irritation in the lungs, further easing symptoms and promoting overall recovery.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory properties: Herbal daisy teas contain flavonoids and phenolic acids that have anti-inflammatory effects, which can help reduce inflammation in the lungs and airways caused by pertussis.

Antimicrobial properties: The tea's antimicrobial compounds, such as sesquiterpenes and terpenes, may help combat bacterial infections like pertussis by inhibiting the growth of Bordetella pertussis bacteria.

Expectorant properties: The expectorant compounds in herbal daisy teas, such as saponins and glycosides, can help loosen and clear mucus from the lungs and airways, making it easier to breathe and reducing congestion associated with pertussis.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots are rich in bioactive compounds that have anti-inflammatory properties, making them effective in soothing coughing fits associated with pertussis.
Bark: The bark contains antioxidants and flavonoids that help to reduce inflammation and fight off bacterial infections, providing relief from pertussis symptoms.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh bellis perennis flowers and leaves from a pesticide-free area early morning or evening.
Rinse the harvested flowers and leaves with cold filtered water to remove dirt and debris immediately.
Combine one teaspoon of dried flowers and leaves or two teaspoons of fresh material with one cup boiling water in a tea infuser slowly.
Steep the mixture for 5-7 minutes, allowing the herbal goodness to seep into the hot water thoroughly.
Strain the tea using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a cup or teapot and serve warm or at room temperature promptly.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Pot marigold teas helps with pertussis because it is rich in antioxidants and anti-inflammatory compounds that help soothe the respiratory tract and reduce inflammation caused by the bacteria. The tea's flavonoids and phenolic acids may also help boost the body's natural defense mechanisms, such as white blood cell production, to combat the infection. Additionally, pot marigold's expectorant properties can help loosen and clear mucus from the airways, making it easier to breathe and aiding in the recovery process.
remedy_properties: Antimicrobial properties: Pot Marigold's antimicrobial compounds, such as saponins and flavonoids, help combat bacterial infections like pertussis by inhibiting the growth of harmful bacteria.

Anti-inflammatory properties: The tea's anti-inflammatory compounds, including sesquiterpenes and triterpenoids, may reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract and alleviate symptoms such as coughing, congestion, and fever associated with pertussis.

Expectorant properties: Pot Marigold's mucilage content helps loosen and clear mucus from the airways, making it easier to expel phlegm and relieving congestion and coughing that can occur during a pertussis infection.
remedy_parts: Roots: Potent anti-inflammatory and antibacterial properties in the roots help to soothe a pertussis cough and reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract.
Seeds: Rich in antioxidants and flavonoids, the seeds of pot marigold plant have been traditionally used to treat respiratory issues like pertussis, reducing inflammation and congestion.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried calendula flowers in sufficient quantities for desired amount of tea.
Measure out two teaspoons of dried calendula flowers or one teaspoon of fresh petals per cup.
Heat filtered water to a boil and let it cool slightly for 5-10 minutes to ideal temperature.
Steep the calendula flowers in hot water for 5-7 minutes, allowing the flavors to infuse slowly.
Strain the tea into a cup using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth, discarding solids and serving hot.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Bell pepper teas helps with pertussis because of its unique blend of antioxidants, vitamins, and minerals that work synergistically to combat the bacterial infection. The tea's high levels of vitamin C and beta-carotene help boost the immune system, while its anti-inflammatory properties reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract, making it easier for the body to expel the bacteria. Additionally, bell pepper teas' natural expectorant properties help loosen and clear mucus, allowing for better coughing up of infected phlegm and ultimately reducing the severity of symptoms.
remedy_properties: Anti-tussive: Bell pepper tea can help alleviate coughing fits associated with pertussis due to its high vitamin C content, which helps reduce inflammation and soothe irritated respiratory tracts.

Expectorant: The tea's active constituents, such as flavonoids and carotenoids, can help loosen and expel mucus from the airways, making it easier to expel phlegm and reduce the severity of pertussis symptoms.

Antioxidant: Bell pepper tea contains powerful antioxidants like vitamin C, vitamin A, and other phytochemicals that can help protect the body from oxidative stress and damage caused by pertussis, thereby supporting the immune system.

Anti-inflammatory: The anti-inflammatory properties of bell pepper tea, attributed to compounds like luteolin and quercetin, can help reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract, providing relief from pertussis-related symptoms.

Immune-boosting: Bell pepper tea is rich in essential vitamins and minerals, such as vitamin C, vitamin A, and potassium, that support and enhance the immune system's ability to fight off pertussis infections.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of bell pepper have been traditionally used to treat respiratory issues, including pertussis, due to their expectorant properties.
Bark: The bark of bell pepper contains compounds that help to reduce inflammation and open up airways, making it effective in soothing symptoms of pertussis.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh bell peppers from your garden or store them in airtight containers for optimal flavor and aroma preservation.
Wash the bell peppers gently with cold running water to remove dirt debris and potential contaminants carefully pat dry.
Chop the bell peppers into small pieces approximately 1-2 inches square using sharp kitchen scissors or knife.
Combine 2 tablespoons of chopped bell pepper with 1 quart of boiling water in a large pot allowing it steep for 10 minutes.
Strain the mixture through a cheesecloth or fine-mesh sieve discarding solids and serving hot tea sweeten as desired with honey or sugar.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Roman chamomile teas helps with pertussis because its soothing and calming properties can provide relief from the intense coughing fits associated with the infection. The apigenin in chamomile has been shown to bind to GABA receptors, promoting relaxation and reducing anxiety, which can be beneficial for individuals experiencing stress and discomfort due to pertussis symptoms. Additionally, chamomile's anti-inflammatory properties may help reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract, further alleviating congestion and coughing.
remedy_properties: Anxiolytic and Sedative Properties: Roman Chamomile tea has been shown to have a calming effect on the nervous system, which can help soothe the anxiety and stress associated with Pertussis, making it easier for patients to rest and recover.

Anti-Inflammatory Properties: The flavonoids present in Roman Chamomile tea have anti-inflammatory properties that may help reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract, which can alleviate symptoms such as coughing and wheezing caused by Pertussis.

Antibacterial and Antiviral Properties: Roman Chamomile tea has been found to exhibit antibacterial and antiviral activity against various microorganisms, including those that cause Pertussis, potentially helping to reduce the severity of the infection.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in essential oils and flavonoids, the roots of Roman Chamomile are valued for their anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties, making them effective in soothing coughs associated with pertussis.

Flowers: The delicate flowers of Roman Chamomile contain apigenin, a flavonoid that has been shown to have antibacterial and anti-inflammatory effects, helping to combat the bacterial infection caused by pertussis.
remedy_recipe: Measure two teaspoons of dried roman chamomile flowers for every eight ounces water.
Bring fresh filtered water to a boil in a medium-sized pot and let it cool for one minute.
Add the measured roman chamomile flowers to the hot water and let steep for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth and discard solids.
Allow the tea to cool to desired temperature and enjoy within 24 hours.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Chicory teas helps with pertussis because its unique blend of antioxidants, flavonoids, and saponins has been shown to boost the immune system and reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract. By calming the cough and relieving congestion, herbal chicory tea can provide much-needed relief for individuals suffering from pertussis, also known as whooping cough. Additionally, the tea's anti-inflammatory properties may help alleviate fever and body aches associated with the condition, promoting a faster recovery.
remedy_properties: Antibacterial compounds: Chicory tea contains antibacterial compounds such as chicoric acid, which have been shown to inhibit the growth of bacteria that cause pertussis, helping to reduce symptoms and alleviate coughing.

Mucilage content: Chicory tea's mucilage content helps soothe and calm irritated respiratory tissues, reducing inflammation and making it easier for patients with pertussis to breathe and cough up mucus.

Expectorant properties: The expectorant properties of chicory tea help loosen and clear out mucus from the lungs and airways, making it easier for patients with pertussis to expel infected mucus and reduce congestion.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in antioxidants and anti-inflammatory compounds, chicory roots help soothe respiratory issues like pertussis.
Seeds: Containing mucilage, a gel-like substance, chicory seeds can form a protective barrier on the mucus membranes, reducing inflammation and congestion caused by pertussis.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried chicory roots cichorium intybus in abundance.
Rinse the roots with clean water to remove dirt and debris thoroughly.
Chop the rinsed roots into small pieces weighing about one ounce per cup.
Steep the chopped roots in boiling water for 10-15 minutes slowly.
Strain the tea leaves and discard them serving hot or chilled afterwards.

supplementary_best_treatment: The best way to treat pertussis with herbal teas is by using a combination of teas known for their antitussive, expectorant, and antispasmodic properties. Look for herbs such as wild cherry bark, licorice root, hyssop, thyme, and coltsfoot. These herbs can help soothe the cough, reduce spasms, and expel mucus from the respiratory tract. Steep 1-2 teaspoons of the herbal blend in boiling water for 10-15 minutes and drink 3-4 cups daily. However, it is crucial to consult a healthcare professional for proper diagnosis and treatment, as herbal teas should complement, not replace, conventional medical care for pertussis.
supplementary_causes: Ailments similar to pertussis that are treated with herbal teas include bronchitis and persistent coughs. For bronchitis, a blend of thyme, licorice, and ivy leaves can be used to alleviate symptoms. Meanwhile, for persistent coughs, a mix of marshmallow root, coltsfoot, and wild cherry bark can provide relief. However, it's important to note that while herbal teas can help manage symptoms, they should not be used as a substitute for medical treatment, especially in severe cases or when dealing with young children or individuals with underlying health conditions. Always consult with a healthcare professional before starting any new treatment.
